lines.greek: παιδὸς γὰρ μῦθον πεπνυμένον ἔνθετο θυμῷ.
ἐς δʼ ὑπερῷʼ ἀναβᾶσα σὺν ἀμφιπόλοισι γυναιξὶ
κλαῖεν ἔπειτʼ Ὀδυσῆα φίλον πόσιν, ὄφρα οἱ ὕπνον
ἡδὺν ἐπὶ βλεφάροισι βάλε γλαυκῶπις Ἀθήνη.
μνηστῆρες δʼ ὁμάδησαν ἀνὰ μέγαρα σκιόεντα,
πάντες δʼ ἠρήσαντο παραὶ λεχέεσσι κλιθῆναι.
τοῖσι δὲ Τηλέμαχος πεπνυμένος ἤρχετο μύθων·
μητρὸς ἐμῆς μνηστῆρες ὑπέρβιον ὕβριν ἔχοντες,
νῦν μὲν δαινύμενοι τερπώμεθα, μηδὲ βοητὺς
ἔστω, ἐπεὶ τόδε καλὸν ἀκουέμεν ἐστὶν ἀοιδοῦ
τοιοῦδʼ οἷος ὅδʼ ἐστί, θεοῖς ἐναλίγκιος αὐδήν.
ἠῶθεν δʼ ἀγορήνδε καθεζώμεσθα κιόντες
πάντες, ἵνʼ ὕμιν μῦθον ἀπηλεγέως ἀποείπω,
ἐξιέναι μεγάρων· ἄλλας δʼ ἀλεγύνετε δαῖτας,
ὑμὰ κτήματʼ ἔδοντες, ἀμειβόμενοι κατὰ οἴκους.
εἰ δʼ ὕμιν δοκέει τόδε λωίτερον καὶ ἄμεινον
ἔμμεναι, ἀνδρὸς ἑνὸς βίοτον νήποινον ὀλέσθαι,
κείρετʼ· ἐγὼ δὲ θεοὺς ἐπιβώσομαι αἰὲν ἐόντας,
αἴ κέ ποθι Ζεὺς δῷσι παλίντιτα ἔργα γενέσθαι·
νήποινοί κεν ἔπειτα δόμων ἔντοσθεν ὄλοισθε.

interlinear: for-her-son's word, prudent, she-laid-up in-her-heart.
and-to-the-upper-chamber having-gone-up with her-handmaid women,
she-wept then for-Odysseus, her-dear husband, until upon-her sleep
sweet on her-eyelids cast bright-eyed Athena.
and-the-wooers made-uproar throughout the-shadowy halls,
and-all prayed beside her-couch to-be-laid.
and-to-them Telemachus, prudent, began his-words:
'of-my-mother wooers, overweening insolence having,
now indeed feasting let-us-take-delight, and-let-there-not shouting
be, since this a-fine-thing to-hear is, of-a-singer
such-a-one as this-man is, to-the-gods like in-voice.
and-at-dawn to-the-assembly let-us-sit, going,
all, so-that to-you my-word outright I-may-declare:
to-go-out from-my-halls. and-other feasts busy-yourselves-with,
your-own goods eating, going-in-turn from house to house.
but-if to-you it-seems this better and more-profitable
to-be, of-one man's livelihood unavenged to-be-destroyed,
waste-on; and-I upon-the-gods will-call, the-ever-existing,
in-case somehow Zeus may-grant deeds-of-requital to-come-about:
unavenged then within the-house you-would-perish.'
literal: for she laid up her son's prudent word in her heart.
She went up to her upper chamber with her handmaid women
and wept there for Odysseus, her dear husband, until
bright-eyed Athena cast sweet sleep upon her eyelids.
But the wooers broke into uproar throughout the shadowy halls,
and all of them prayed to lie beside her.
And prudent Telemachus was first to speak among them:
'Wooers of my mother, overweening in your insolence,
let us take our delight in the feast now, and let there be
no shouting, since it is a fine thing to hear such a singer
as this man is, like the gods in his voice.
But at dawn let us all go and sit in assembly,
that I may declare my word to you outright:
to go out from my halls. Busy yourselves with other feasts,
eating your own goods, going from house to house in turn.
But if it seems to you better and more profitable
that one man's livelihood be destroyed without atonement,
then waste on. And I will call upon the gods who are forever,
in the hope that Zeus may grant deeds of requital:
then you would perish within this house unavenged.'
translations.elizabethan:
She laid her son's wise saying up in mind,
and to her upper chamber went, and there,
her women with her, wept Odysseus,
her own dear husband, till the bright-eyed one,
Athena, cast sweet sleep upon her lids.
But through the shadowy hall the suitors broke
in uproar, praying each to lie by her.
Then prudent Telemachus began:
"Suitors upon my mother, insolent
beyond all measure, let us take our joy
in feasting now, and let there be no shouting,
since it is good to hear a singer such
as this man is, in voice much like the gods.
But when the dawn comes, let us all go sit
in the assembly, that I may declare
my word out plainly: get you from my halls.
Busy yourselves with other feasts than mine,
eat your own goods, go house by house in turn.
But if it seem to you the better way,
and more to profit, that one man's whole living
be eaten up and never paid for, then
devour it. I will call upon the gods
that live for ever, that Zeus grant at last
requital: and unavenged within these walls
you shall be slain."
translations.modernist:
She took her son's sensible words and kept them.

She went upstairs with her women and cried for Odysseus, her husband, until bright-eyed Athena put sweet sleep on her eyelids.

The suitors made a racket through the shadowy hall. Every one of them prayed to get into her bed.

Then Telemachus spoke first. He had sense.

"Suitors of my mother. You are insolent men and there is no limit to it. Enjoy the feast now. But no shouting. It is a good thing to listen to a singer like this one. His voice is like the voices of the gods.

In the morning we will all go and sit in the assembly. I am going to say it to you straight. Get out of my house. Feast somewhere else. Eat your own food. Go from one house to the next.

But if you think it is better business to eat up one man's living and never pay for it, then go on eating. I will call on the gods, who go on forever. I hope Zeus grants me a payment in kind. Then you will die in this house, and nobody will avenge you."
translations.storybook:
She kept her son's sensible words in her heart.

Then she went upstairs with her women, and cried and cried for Odysseus, her dear husband, until bright-eyed Athena laid sweet sleep upon her eyelids.

But down below, the suitors made a great racket all through the shadowy hall, and every single one of them was praying that he might be the one to lie beside her.

Then sensible young Telemachus spoke up first.

"Suitors of my mother! There is no end to your rudeness. Very well, enjoy your feast now, but stop all this shouting, because it is a fine thing to listen to a singer like this one, whose voice is as lovely as a god's.

But in the morning we shall all go and sit in the assembly, and I am going to tell you straight out: get out of my house! Go and have your feasts somewhere else. Eat up your own food, and go round from one house to the next.

But if you think it is a better bargain to eat one man's whole living and never pay a penny for it, then go on and eat. I shall call upon the gods who live for ever, and I hope with all my heart that Zeus grants me payment in kind. Then you will die right here in this house, and nobody will avenge you."

murray.text: But the wooers broke into uproar throughout the shadowy halls, and all prayed, each that he might lie by her side. And among them wise Telemachus was the first to speak: “Wooers of my mother, overweening in your insolence, for the present let us make merry with feasting,

but let there be no brawling; for this is a goodly thing, to listen to a minstrel such as this man is, like to the gods in voice. But in the morning let us go to the assembly and take our seats, one and all, that I may declare my word to you outright that you depart from these halls. Prepare you other feasts,

eating your own substance and changing from house to house. But if this seems in your eyes to be a better and more profitable thing, that one man's livelihood should be ruined without atonement, waste ye it. But I will call upon the gods that are forever, if haply Zeus may grant that deeds of requital may be wrought.

Without atonement, then, should ye perish within my halls.” So he spoke, and they all bit their lips and marvelled at Telemachus, for that he spoke boldly. Then Antinous, son of Eupeithes, answered him:“Telemachus, verily the gods themselves are teaching thee
